message.content: HTML Smuggling is a technique where adversaries hide malicious payloads within seemingly benign HTML files to bypass content filters. This method leverages JavaScript Blobs and Data URLs to embed and later reconstruct malicious files. HTML5's download attribute can also be exploited to initiate file downloads. Security controls often fail to detect these smuggled files as they appear to be standard MIME types like text/plain or text/html. Once the HTML file reaches the victim, the malicious content can be deobfuscated and executed, potentially bypassing security measures. Notable examples include APT29 embedding an ISO file within an HTML attachment and EnvyScout using JavaScript to extract and write an encoded blob to disk. Mitigation strategies include implementing advanced content filtering that can inspect and analyze embedded objects within HTML files, monitoring for unusual file download behaviors, and educating users about the risks of opening unexpected HTML attachments.
